impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's patent-based defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Clarifies that competitors can bring antitrust counterclaims when patent holders leverage patents to force tied purchases, providing a meaningful remedy against anticompetitive patent licensing practices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk if they condition patent licenses on exclusive purchases of unpatented products, even when those products are related to the patented technology. Businesses must carefully structure licensing agreements to avoid tying claims.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model are abstract and ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 and the Alice framework.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents covering virus detection via emulation were abstract at Alice step one. The court also reversed the district court's denial of JMOL on damages for foreign sales, finding insufficient basis for recovering damages based on Norton's sales to customers outside the United States.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides technology companies with a stronger basis to challenge cybersecurity-related software patents under § 101, and limits patent holders' ability to recover damages based on foreign sales without adequate nexus to U.S. infringement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in software and cybersecurity fields face heightened eligibility risks, as method claims involving comparison and detection steps may be deemed abstract. Universities and research institutions monetizing software patents face increased invalidation risk.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims covering server-side tailoring of content presentation specifications to a wireless device's capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods of tailoring streamed content to wireless device capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment after a jury had found infringement and awarded $2.5 million. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content to device characteristics, requiring reversal of the judgment in Netflix's favor.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies benefit from expanded § 101 protections against patents claiming broad methods of content adaptation for different devices, reducing litigation exposure in this common technology area.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to content delivery optimization and device-tailored streaming face significant eligibility challenges. This decision narrows the scope of enforceable patents in the digital content delivery space.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was appropriate on inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims related to failure to disclose prior art (CYMAX Documents) during patent prosecution.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact. Tenaris acquired prior art documents (CYMAX Documents) describing a similar coiled tubing product before prosecuting its patents but allegedly failed to disclose them to the PTO. The court remanded both the inequitable conduct defense and the attempted monopolization counterclaim for trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors challenging patents obtained through alleged fraud have a clearer path to trial on both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust claims, providing meaningful remedies against patent holders who suppress material prior art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies acquiring prior art through asset purchases face heightened risk that failure to disclose those materials during patent prosecution could support both unenforceability and antitrust liability, including treble damages under the Sherman Act.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to cultured host cells containing recombinant nucleic acid molecules encoding an AAV capsid protein with a heterologous non-AAV sequence are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 or directed to a natural phenomenon.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed summary judgment of patent ineligibility, holding that claims to genetically engineered host cells containing specific recombinant nucleic acid molecules for gene therapy are not directed to a natural phenomenon. The court found the claimed cultured host cells are human-made constructs that do not occur in nature, preserving the validity of an important gene therapy patent portfolio.
impacts.positiveImplications: Biotech and pharmaceutical companies gain stronger protection for gene therapy patents, particularly claims to engineered host cells and recombinant constructs. This decision supports patentability of key enabling technologies in the growing gene therapy field.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in gene therapy may face continued enforcement of broad patent claims covering engineered host cells containing AAV sequences, potentially increasing licensing costs and barriers to entry in this therapeutic space.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a respondent in an ITC proceeding may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action after having missed the deadline for seeking a stay of its originally filed action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action by invoking § 1659(a)(2) after having missed the 30-day deadline in the original action. The court found that allowing a party to circumvent the statutory deadline by refiling the same action would undermine the purpose of the time limitations Congress established.
impacts.positiveImplications: Declaratory judgment plaintiffs (e.g., accused infringers) gain certainty that opponents cannot manipulate mandatory stay provisions by refiling actions, preserving timely access to district court adjudication of patent disputes parallel to ITC proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC complainants who miss the 30-day mandatory stay deadline cannot cure the deficiency by having the opposing party refile the same action, losing the ability to delay parallel district court litigation.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement functionality violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watches incorporating blood oxygen measurement features covered by Masimo's patents. The court upheld the ITC's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, and infringement findings, sustaining the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing Apple Watch models.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device and wearable technology space receive strong confirmation that the ITC remains an effective forum for enforcing patents against major importers, even for consumer electronics incorporating health-monitoring features.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies integrating patented health-monitoring features into consumer devices face significant import exclusion risk. Apple faces continued restrictions on importing certain Apple Watch models, impacting a major product line.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft. The district court had excluded the expert's report and then granted summary judgment on the basis that Exafer could not prove damages. The Federal Circuit found the exclusion was an abuse of discretion and that the resulting summary judgment based solely on absence of a remedy was improper.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more protective standard for damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that exclusion of a damages expert automatically results in case dismissal.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent suits face reduced ability to obtain summary judgment by targeting damages experts, as courts must give plaintiffs adequate opportunity to present damages evidence.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denial of inter partes review petitions constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary non-institution of IPR proceedings are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under the APA. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the instructions do not bind the Director, and non-institution decisions are insulated from judicial review.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains broad flexibility to issue and modify guidance on IPR institution discretion without formal rulemaking, allowing efficient administration of the patent review system.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies seeking IPR as a tool to challenge patents face continued uncertainty about institution, as the Director's non-institution policies can change without public participation and remain largely unreviewable.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million class settlement resolving claims that PACER fees were unlawfully used to fund projects beyond the system's operation, in violation of the E-Government Act. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fee awards, and incentive awards to class representatives, noting that hundreds of thousands of PACER users will be substantially reimbursed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are frequent PACER users will receive reimbursement for overcharged fees. The settlement establishes accountability for government fee-setting practices under the E-Government Act.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims containing a means-plus-function 'control means' limitation are indefinite when the specification discloses the corresponding structure as 'conventional in design and operation' without detailed structural specifics.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that asserted claims of a crop harvester patent were invalid as indefinite. The court held that the specification's disclosure of a controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system as 'conventional in design and operation' was sufficient to identify the corresponding structure for the means-plus-function limitation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a more forgiving standard for specification disclosure, particularly when corresponding structures are described as conventional. This preserves enforceability of patents in agricultural and industrial technology.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face a higher bar to invalidate means-plus-function claims for indefiniteness, even when the specification provides limited structural detail for conventional components.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced patent infringement and in granting JMOL for the defendant in a medical device patent case.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and its grant of JMOL for DePuy in a patent case involving spinal surgery derotation tools. The court found that the expert testimony was improperly excluded and that JMOL was erroneously granted, remanding for a new trial where both experts may testify.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders, particularly individual inventors and small entities, benefit from broader latitude for expert testimony on induced infringement, increasing their ability to present cases against large medical device companies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed trial exposure on induced infringement claims. The decision signals that courts should be cautious in excluding experts whose methodology may be challenged but is not fundamentally unreliable.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of multimedia presentations are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement action against Google, holding that claims covering web conferencing systems allowing asynchronous viewing of recorded content were ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of recording and playing back multimedia content.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating web conferencing and video streaming platforms gain protection from broad patent claims covering basic time-shifting and asynchronous playback functionality.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims to web conferencing and multimedia playback systems face continued § 101 eligibility challenges, limiting monetization of patents in the collaboration technology space.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents rather than a general exclusion order, and whether Crocs's appeal of the no-violation finding as to active respondents was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's untimely appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding as to active respondents and affirmed the limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. The ITC had found no trademark infringement or dilution of Crocs's 3D trade dress marks by active respondents, while entering a limited (not general) exclusion order against defaulting parties under § 337(g)(1).
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and retailers of competing footwear products benefit from the ITC's finding that Crocs's 3D trade dress marks were not infringed, limiting Crocs's ability to exclude competitive products at the border.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking broad import exclusion protection through ITC proceedings face procedural and substantive barriers, including strict appeal deadlines and high evidentiary burdens for proving trade dress likelihood of confusion.
